macro_segments.title: Moshe's Status as a Kohen: Conceptual Foundations and the Debate Among Tannaim

micro_segments.title: Introduction: The Question of Whether Moshe Was a Kohen, and the Context of Miriam's Tzara'at
Conceptual Foundations: Kehunah as a Status Not Limited to Descendants of Aharon; Examples of Malki Tzedek and Yitro
Pinchas as a Counterexample: A Descendant of Aharon Who Was Not Initially a Kohen
The Meisivei Challenge: Moshe Was a Zar (Non-Kohen) Because He Could Not Examine Negaim
Rav Nachman bar Yitzchak's Qualification: The Negaim Examination Requires Descent from Aharon Specifically

macro_segments.title: The Elisheva Narrative and the Status of Pinchas: Was Pinchas Already a Kohen Before the Zimri Event?

micro_segments.title: Elisheva's Five Sources of Joy: Family Members' Positions and the Implied Status of Pinchas
The Challenge: If Pinchas Was Already a Kohen, Why Wasn't He Designated Mashuach Milchamah Earlier?

macro_segments.title: The Tannaitic Debate: Was Moshe Ever a Kohen, or Only During the Miluim Period?

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon ben Yochai's Interpretation: God's Anger Against Moshe Manifested in Separating the Roles—Moshe as Kohen, Aharon as Levi
The Chachamim's Position: Moshe Served as Kohen Only During the Seven Days of Miluim, Without Prior Designation
Rav's Position: Moshe Was a Kohen, But His Kehunah Did Not Pass to His Descendants

macro_segments.title: Anger, Respect, and Kingship: Philosophical Exploration of Divine and Human Authority

micro_segments.title: The Principle of Charon Af: Every Instance of Divine Anger in Torah Leaves a Mark on Its Target
The Debate: Whether Moshe's Departure from Pharaoh Demonstrates the Mark of Charon Af
Rabbi Yannai's Teaching: The Fear of Kings Must Always Be Maintained, Regardless of Righteousness
Ulla's Discussion: Moshe Sought Kingship (Hereditary Rule) But Was Not Granted It
The Nature of Halom (Kingdom): Kingship as a Hereditary Status Granted for All Future Generations

macro_segments.title: Ba'alei Mumim (Those with Blemishes): Entitlement to Portions Despite Inability to Perform Avoda

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah's Rule: Anyone Not Fit for Avoda Does Not Receive a Portion
Textual Derivation: Three Instances of 'Kol Zachar' (All Males) Include Ba'alei Mumim in the Portions
The Interpretive Problem: Determining Whether the Rule Is About Avoda or Achila (Eating)
Exceptions in Both Directions: Ba'alei Mumim Can't Perform Avoda But Get Portions; Some Who Can Perform Avoda Don't Get Portions
Resolution: Read the Mishnah as About Achila; Don't Infer the Reverse Case

macro_segments.title: Tumah and Tahara: The Moment When Status Determines Entitlement to Portions

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah: Purity Status at the Time of Dam (Blood) Determines Portion Entitlement, Regardless of Status at Haktaras Chalavim
Abba Shaul's Stringent Position: Purity Is Required at Both Dam and Haktaras Chalavim
Rav Ashi's Unresolved Question: Does Abba Shaul Require Purity Throughout, or Only at Two Points?

macro_segments.title: The Tvul Yom's Plea: A Narrative Exploration of Exclusion via Kal VaComer Arguments

micro_segments.title: The Tvul Yom Requests a Portion from the Mincha; Rebuttal Via Kal VaComer on Chatas and Mincha
The Tvul Yom Pivots to Chatas; Further Kal VaComer Comparing Rights in Chatas vs. Shelamim
The Tvul Yom Requests Chaze and Shok from Shelamim; Final Kal VaComer on Rights in Shelamim
The Tvul Yom's Departure: Dejected, with Kal VaComer Arguments Piled Upon His Head

macro_segments.title: Rav Achai's Additional Kal VaComer: The Bechor Case and Its Peculiarity

micro_segments.title: Rav Achai's Challenge: Why Didn't the Tvul Yom Request a Bechor?
Tosafot's Academic Note: Rav Achai's Questions Sometimes Appear Textually Anomalous

macro_segments.title: The Bathroom Boundary: Torah as Transgressive Force vs. Korbanot as Exclusionary Space

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's Question: How Could Rabbi Elazar Contemplate Torah in the Bathroom?
The Principle: Contemplation Is Forbidden in the Bathhouse and Bathroom, Except When One Cannot Control It
Theological Contrast: Torah Invades All Spaces (Including the Bathroom), While Korbanot Maintains Rigid Boundaries and Exclusions
